drugs.name: Cisplatin/Etoposide (preferred)
drugs.dose: Cisplatin 75 mg/m² Day 1 and Etoposide 100 mg/m² Days 1, 2, 3

biomarkers.requirement: Clinical stage I-IIA (T1-2,N0,M0) with negative pathologic lymph node staging required before surgery

trial_data.os_rates: 5-year survival 40%-60% in patients with stage I disease

regimens.notes: Fewer than 5% of SCLC patients have true stage I-IIA disease [84]. Preferred operation is lobectomy with mediastinal lymph node dissection or systematic lymph node sampling (≥3 N2 and ≥1 N1 stations) [3-7]. Pneumonectomy should not be performed if needed to encompass nodal metastatic disease. Patients with N+ disease should receive concurrent or sequential chemoradiation. Patients with N1 may be considered for postoperative mediastinal radiation [97]. Small lesions presumed to be SCLC in non-smokers should be resected as they are likely misdiagnosed carcinoids. The Lung Cancer Study Group prospective randomized trial (Lad et al.) showed no benefit to surgery in patients who had responded to induction chemotherapy [92].

regimens.regimen_name: Stereotactic Ablative Radiotherapy (SABR) followed by Adjuvant Systemic Therapy
regimens.setting: Primary treatment for medically inoperable or inoperable stage I-IIA

drugs.name: SABR to primary tumor
drugs.dose: Per SABR principles (see NCCN NSCLC guidelines)

biomarkers.requirement: Good PS required for adjuvant systemic therapy

trial_data.os_median: 31.4 months with SABR + chemotherapy vs. 14.3 months SABR alone (P=.02) [271]; 29.2 months SABR/chemotherapy vs. 31.2 months concurrent chemoradiation (P=.77) [10]

regimens.notes: SABR (also known as SBRT) is an option for select patients with clinically limited-stage I-IIA (T1-2,N0) SCLC who are medically inoperable or decline surgery [12,271]. Systemic therapy may be initiated first if time to initiation of SABR will be prolonged. 44% of patients in meta-analysis received chemotherapy and 13.8% received PCI [270]. Both ASTRO and the American Radium Society recommend SABR followed by adjuvant chemotherapy for medically inoperable patients [236,237]. Principles of SABR for SCLC are similar to those for NSCLC.

regimens.regimen_name: Cisplatin/Etoposide + Concurrent Radiation Therapy (45 Gy BID)
regimens.setting: Primary treatment for limited-stage IIB-IIIC (T3-4,N0,M0; T1-4,N1-3,M0)

biomarkers.requirement: Good PS (0-2) required for concurrent chemoradiation

trial_data.os_median: 30 months (45 Gy BID) vs. 25 months (66 Gy QD) [105]
trial_data.orr: 70%-90% response rates with cisplatin/etoposide + thoracic RT [105,106]

regimens.notes: Four cycles of cytotoxic chemotherapy are recommended. Planned cycle length should be every 21-28 days during concurrent RT. RT should start early, with cycle 1 or 2 of systemic therapy (category 1) [10]. A shorter time from the start of any therapy to the end of RT (SER) is significantly associated with improved survival [11]. Accelerated 45 Gy in 3 weeks (1.5 Gy twice daily) is category 1 based on INT 0096 [19,20]. When BID fractionation is used, there should be at least a 6-hour interfraction interval. Use of myeloid growth factors is not recommended during concurrent cytotoxic chemotherapy plus RT (category 1 for not using GM-CSF) [1]. IMRT is preferred over 3D-CRT [1].

regimens.regimen_name: Cisplatin/Etoposide + Concurrent Radiation Therapy (66-70 Gy QD)
regimens.setting: Primary treatment for limited-stage IIB-IIIC (T3-4,N0,M0; T1-4,N1-3,M0)
regimens.notes: Higher doses of 66-70 Gy are preferred if using once-daily fractionation. Two randomized phase 3 trials did not demonstrate superiority of 66-70 Gy over 45 Gy BID, but OS and toxicity were similar [28-30]. Recent phase 2/3 trials suggest high-dose accelerated RT (54-65 Gy) may produce improved OS or PFS compared to standard 45 Gy BID [31-34]. The CONVERT trial compared 45 Gy BID vs. 66 Gy QD [28]. CALGB 30610/RTOG 0538 compared 70 Gy QD vs. 45 Gy BID [29].

regimens.regimen_name: Cisplatin/Etoposide + Concurrent Radiation Therapy (60 mg/m² cisplatin)

trial_data.os_median: 23 months (45 Gy BID) vs. 19 months (45 Gy QD) (P=.04) [3]

regimens.notes: This cisplatin/etoposide dosing is based on the regimen used in the Turrisi et al. 1999 trial [3]. The 45 Gy BID over 3 weeks is category 1 superior to 45 Gy QD over 5 weeks. A shorter time from start of therapy to end of RT is associated with improved survival.

regimens.setting: Primary treatment for limited-stage IIB-IIIC (when cisplatin contraindicated or not tolerated)
regimens.notes: Carboplatin/etoposide is listed as a preferred option for limited-stage SCLC based on the subgroup analysis in the ADRIATIC trial [115,116]. This is listed as an option when cisplatin is contraindicated or not tolerated [footnote a]. Use of carboplatin carries a greater risk of myelosuppression compared to cisplatin [104].

regimens.regimen_name: Cisplatin/Etoposide + Concurrent Radiation Therapy (25 mg/m² cisplatin Days 1-3)
regimens.notes: This is a split-dose cisplatin regimen with both cisplatin and etoposide administered on days 1, 2, and 3. Listed as an Other Recommended option for limited-stage SCLC. Four cycles are recommended.

regimens.setting: Adjuvant/consolidation for limited-stage SCLC after completion of systemic therapy + concurrent RT with no disease progression

drugs.name: Durvalumab
drugs.schedule: Day 1 every 28 days for up to 24 months

biomarkers.requirement: No disease progression after systemic therapy + concurrent RT

regimens.notes: Category 1 recommendation based on ADRIATIC trial [5]. Durvalumab may be continued until disease progression or intolerable toxicity, or for a maximum of 24 months. Imaging every 8 weeks for the first 18 months and every 12 weeks after [footnote aa]. If PCI is considered, it should be given prior to durvalumab [footnote y]. For those with good PS who are medically inoperable or decision was made not to pursue surgical resection [footnote z].

regimens.regimen_name: Carboplatin/Etoposide + Atezolizumab followed by Maintenance Atezolizumab (1200 mg Q3W)
regimens.setting: Primary treatment for extensive-stage SCLC (without localized symptomatic sites or brain metastases)

drugs.name: Atezolizumab
drugs.schedule: Day 1, every 21 days x 4 cycles (induction), then maintenance 1200 mg Day 1 every 21 days

biomarkers.requirement: Not required; used regardless of PD-L1 status

trial_data.os_median: 12.3 months (atezolizumab + chemo) vs. 10.3 months (chemo alone) (HR, 0.76; 95% CI, 0.6-0.95; P=.0154) [118]

regimens.notes: Category 1 for all components. Four cycles of cytotoxic chemotherapy are recommended, but some patients may receive up to 6 cycles based on response and tolerability [118,120]. Grade 3-4 adverse events: 67.7% (atezolizumab regimen) vs. 63.3% (chemo alone). Deaths: 4 (2%) in atezolizumab group vs. 11 (5.6%) in chemo alone group. Contraindications for PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitors include active or previously documented autoimmune disease and/or concurrent use of immunosuppressive agents [footnote d]. Maintenance immunotherapy should continue until progression or intolerable toxicity [footnote e]. Atezolizumab and hyaluronidase-tqjs subcutaneous injection may be substituted for IV atezolizumab [footnote f].

regimens.regimen_name: Carboplatin/Etoposide + Atezolizumab followed by Maintenance Atezolizumab (1680 mg Q4W)

drugs.name: Atezolizumab
drugs.schedule: Induction: Day 1 every 21 days x 4 cycles; Maintenance: 1680 mg Day 1 every 28 days

regimens.notes: The 1680 mg every 28 days dose is an FDA-approved alternative schedule for maintenance atezolizumab. Category 1 recommendation is only for the 1200 mg dose based on the dose used in the clinical trial. Both atezolizumab and hyaluronidase-tqjs subcutaneous injection formulations are available.

regimens.regimen_name: Carboplatin/Etoposide + Atezolizumab followed by Maintenance Lurbinectedin + Atezolizumab
regimens.setting: Primary treatment for extensive-stage SCLC (maintenance phase)

biomarkers.requirement: At least stable disease following 4 cycles of induction chemoimmunotherapy

trial_data.pfs_median: Stratified HR, 0.54 (95% CI, 0.43-0.67; P<.0001) for PFS

regimens.notes: Category 1 for all components. Consider adding lurbinectedin to maintenance atezolizumab in patients who have achieved at least stable disease following 4 cycles of induction chemoimmunotherapy, have an ECOG PS of 0-1, and no history of brain metastases [footnote g]. This regimen is not for use as re-treatment in subsequent-line therapy if lurbinectedin has been used previously [footnote h]. Patients in IMforte were randomized after platinum therapy only if they had response or stable disease; <10% of patients on the control arm received lurbinectedin after progression [7].

regimens.regimen_name: Carboplatin/Etoposide + Durvalumab followed by Maintenance Durvalumab

drugs.name: Durvalumab
drugs.schedule: Induction: Day 1 every 21 days x 4 cycles; Maintenance: Day 1 every 28 days

biomarkers.requirement: Included patients with asymptomatic untreated brain metastases [footnote i]

trial_data.os_median: 13.0 months (durvalumab + chemo) vs. 10.3 months (chemo alone) (HR, 0.73; 95% CI, 0.59-0.91; P=.0047) [124]

regimens.notes: Category 1 for all components. Most patients in CASPIAN received carboplatin (78%) [119,123]. Serious adverse events: 32% (durvalumab regimen) vs. 36% (chemo alone). Death rate from adverse events: 2% vs. 1%. Adding tremelimumab did not improve OS (10.4 vs. 10.5 months; HR, 0.82; 95% CI, 0.68-1.0) [123]. Included patients with asymptomatic untreated brain metastases [footnote i]. Contraindications for PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitors include autoimmune disease and/or immunosuppressive agents [footnote d].

regimens.regimen_name: Cisplatin/Etoposide + Durvalumab followed by Maintenance Durvalumab

drugs.name: Durvalumab
drugs.schedule: Induction: Day 1 every 21 days x 4 cycles; Maintenance: Day 1 every 28 days

regimens.notes: Category 1 for all components. Alternative to the carboplatin-based durvalumab regimen. Based on the CASPIAN trial which allowed either carboplatin or cisplatin. The cisplatin regimen is preferred for patients without contraindications to cisplatin.

Other recommended regimen for extensive-stage SCLC when immunotherapy is not indicated or not tolerated. Four cycles recommended, though some patients may receive up to 6 cycles based on response and tolerability. Carboplatin carries greater risk of myelosuppression compared to cisplatin.
Other recommended first-line regimen for extensive-stage SCLC when immunotherapy is not indicated. Cisplatin/etoposide is the most commonly used combination chemotherapy regimen for SCLC in general.
Alternative cisplatin/etoposide dosing for extensive-stage SCLC. This dosing is similar to that used in the Niell et al. CLB 9732 trial [11].
Split-dose cisplatin regimen for extensive-stage SCLC. Based on historical data [12].

Useful in certain circumstances for extensive-stage SCLC. A phase 3 randomized trial of 220 patients found median OS slightly improved with carboplatin/irinotecan compared with carboplatin/oral etoposide (8.5 vs. 7.1 months; P=.04) [129]. Meta-analysis suggested improvement in PFS and OS with irinotecan/platinum vs. etoposide/platinum [130]. The relatively small absolute survival benefit needs to be balanced against toxicity. If using irinotecan for patients with CNS disease, irinotecan may be preferred [footnote m].

Useful in certain circumstances. The Japanese JCOG trial showed improved OS with cisplatin/irinotecan vs. cisplatin/etoposide [126]. However, two subsequent large US phase 3 trials showed no significant difference [127,128]. Meta-analysis suggested improvement with irinotecan/platinum [130].
Useful in certain circumstances. Based on the Hanna et al. trial comparing irinotecan/cisplatin with etoposide/cisplatin in patients with previously untreated extensive-stage SCLC [15,128].

regimens.setting: Subsequent therapy (second-line and beyond) for relapsed/refractory SCLC (PS 0-2)

drugs.name: Tarlatamab-dlle
drugs.schedule: Per prescribing information (bispecific T-cell engager targeting DLL3 and CD3)

biomarkers.requirement: DLL3 is abnormally expressed on the surface of the majority of SCLC cells (85-94%); specific testing requirement not specified in guidelines

regimens.notes: Category 1 preferred regimen. Tarlatamab is a bispecific T-cell engager antibody targeting delta-like ligand 3 (DLL3) and CD3 [195]. DLL3 is expressed on 85-94% of SCLC cells. Grade 3+ adverse events: 54% (tarlatamab) vs. 80% (chemotherapy). Cytokine release syndrome observed in 56% of patients. Refer to NCCN Guidelines for Management of CAR T-Cell and Lymphocyte Engager-Related Toxicities [footnote l]. For extensive stage with disease progression on or after platinum-based chemotherapy [footnote k].

regimens.setting: Subsequent therapy (second-line and beyond) for relapsed/refractory SCLC

trial_data.orr: 47% response rate in phase 2 study (95% CI, 21.4%-1.9%) [197]

regimens.notes: Preferred subsequent therapy option. Phase 2 study showed 47% response rate in relapsed/refractory SCLC [197]. Phase 2/3 trial showed comparable activity of irinotecan vs. topotecan [198]. For patients with CNS disease, consider using irinotecan [footnote m].

regimens.setting: Subsequent therapy (second-line) for relapsed/refractory SCLC (if not previously used)

trial_data.os_median: 16.2 months (95% CI, 9.6-upper level not reached) in subset analysis [200]
trial_data.orr: 35% overall (95% CI, 26.2%-45.2%) [199]; 60% in subset of candidates for rechallenge (95% CI, 36.1%-86.9%) [200]

regimens.notes: Preferred subsequent therapy option. Must not have been previously used. 57% of patients in the phase 2 trial had not received chemotherapy for ≥3 months [199]. Common grade 3-4 adverse events: anemia, leukopenia, neutropenia, thrombocytopenia. No treatment-related deaths reported. FDA approved based on clinical trial data.

regimens.setting: Subsequent therapy for relapsed SCLC with prolonged disease-free time

trial_data.pfs_median: 4.7 months (carboplatin/etoposide) vs. 2.7 months (oral topotecan) (90% CI, 3.9-5.5 vs. 2.3-3.2)

regimens.notes: Preferred subsequent therapy option if there has been prolonged disease-free time. Retreatment with the original regimen with or without immunotherapy, or similar platinum-based regimen, is recommended [201-205]. Grade 3-4 adverse events included thrombocytopenia, neutropenia, anemia, febrile neutropenia, and asthenia. Two treatment-related deaths in the topotecan group; no deaths in the carboplatin/etoposide group.

regimens.setting: Subsequent therapy (second-line and beyond) for relapsed/refractory SCLC

drugs.name: Topotecan
drugs.schedule: Per prescribing information (IV or oral formulations available)

trial_data.trial_name: von Pawel et al. (IV topotecan vs. CAV); O'Brien et al. (oral topotecan vs. BSC); Eckardt et al. (oral vs. IV)
trial_data.os_median: 25.0 weeks (topotecan) vs. 24.7 weeks (CAV) [26]; 26 weeks (oral topotecan) vs. 14 weeks (BSC) [27]

regimens.notes: Preferred subsequent therapy option. Efficacy and toxicity of oral and IV topotecan appear similar, and either route may be used [210,211]. Compared to CAV, topotecan caused less grade 4 neutropenia (37.8% vs. 51.4%; P<.001) and improved symptoms of dyspnea, anorexia, hoarseness, and fatigue [26]. Many oncologists note excessive toxicity with 1.5 mg/m² IV for 5 days; attenuated dosing may be equally efficacious with lower toxicity [209]. Oral topotecan improved OS compared with BSC (26 vs. 14 weeks) [27].

Other recommended subsequent therapy option. CAV has similar efficacy to topotecan but with more grade 4 neutropenia and worse symptom profiles [26]. Specific drug doses are not provided in the NCCN SCLC guideline.

trial_data.orr: 25% response rate in phase 2 trial in previously treated SCLC [218]

regimens.notes: Other recommended subsequent therapy option. Phase 2 study showed 25% response rate in previously treated SCLC [218]. Reported toxicities included neutropenia and asthenia.

trial_data.trial_name: Phase 2 (ECOG 1597, van der Lee et al., Hoang et al.)
trial_data.os_median: 7.1 months (ECOG 1597); 17 weeks (van der Lee et al.)

regimens.notes: Other recommended subsequent therapy option. Phase 2 trial in 42 patients showed 11.9% ORR and median OS 7.1 months [30]. Another trial in 38 resistant SCLC patients showed 13% ORR [31,32]. Grade 3-4 toxicities include neutropenia, thrombocytopenia, neurologic toxicity, and pulmonary toxicity.

regimens.setting: Subsequent therapy for relapsed/refractory SCLC (if not previously treated with ICI)

trial_data.os_median: 7.5 months (nivolumab) vs. 8.4 months (chemotherapy) in CM331 (HR, 0.86; 95% CI, 0.72-1.04; P=.11) [213]

regimens.notes: Other recommended subsequent therapy option for patients not previously treated with ICI. CheckMate 032 showed 11.6% ORR and 21.9% for ipilimumab + nivolumab [221,222]. CheckMate 331 showed no significant OS improvement (HR, 0.86; P=.11) [213]. FDA has withdrawn subsequent therapy indications for nivolumab based on phase 3 data not showing OS improvement. Nivolumab and hyaluronidase-nvhy subcutaneous injection may be substituted for IV nivolumab [footnote n]. The use of nivolumab is discouraged if disease progresses on maintenance atezolizumab or durvalumab as first-line therapy.

regimens.setting: Subsequent therapy for relapsed/refractory SCLC (if not previously treated with ICI)
regimens.notes: Other recommended subsequent therapy option for patients not previously treated with ICI. Combined analysis of KEYNOTE-028 and KEYNOTE-158 showed 19.3% ORR and median OS 7.7 months in patients who had received ≥2 prior lines [226]. OS and ORR were higher in PD-L1 positive patients. Grade 3-5 adverse events in 9.6% (8/83) of patients; two treatment-related deaths (pneumonitis and encephalitis). FDA has withdrawn subsequent therapy indication. Pembrolizumab and berahyaluronidase alfa-pmph subcutaneous injection may be substituted for IV pembrolizumab [footnote o]. Use is discouraged if disease progresses on first-line maintenance ICI.

trial_data.trial_name: Phase 2 trials (Einhorn et al., Johnson et al.)

regimens.notes: Other recommended subsequent therapy option. Phase 2 trial in 22 patients showed 45% ORR (95% CI, 27%-65%) [37]. Five patients were hospitalized for neutropenia and fever; two died from sepsis. Another trial showed 23% ORR [38].

trial_data.trial_name: Phase 2 trials (Yamamoto et al., Smit et al.)

regimens.notes: Other recommended subsequent therapy option. Phase 2 study showed 24% response rate in refractory/relapsed SCLC [39]. Another phase 2 study showed 29% ORR (95% CI, 12%-51%) [40]. A retrospective study in 185 patients showed 17% ORR during third-line or fourth-line therapy with paclitaxel [217]. Toxicity rates were similar in PS 2 vs. PS 0-1 (63% vs. 62%) [217].

biomarkers.requirement: Methylated O6-methylguanine-DNA methyltransferase (MGMT) may be associated with improved response

trial_data.trial_name: Phase 2 (Pietanza et al., Zauderer et al.)
trial_data.orr: 23% (95% CI, 12%-37%) in sensitive SCLC [40]; ORR 38% with methylated MGMT vs. 7% unmethylated (P=.08) [40]

regimens.notes: Other recommended subsequent therapy option. Data suggest temozolomide may be useful for patients with SCLC, especially those with brain metastases and methylated MGMT [40-42]. Phase 2 trial showed 23% ORR (95% CI, 12%-37%) in sensitive SCLC, with higher response in methylated MGMT (38% vs. 7%; P=.08).

regimens.regimen_name: Platinum-Based Therapy for Transformed SCLC from NSCLC with Oncogenic Driver
regimens.site: Small cell lung cancer (SCLC) - Transformed from NSCLC with oncogenic driver

biomarkers.requirement: Documented oncogenic driver mutation in prior NSCLC

trial_data.trial_name: Retrospective series (Marcoux et al., Ferrer et al., Chai et al., Zhang et al.)

regimens.notes: This is a rare population of patients with very limited data to guide treatment [43-46]. Systemic cytotoxic chemotherapy is recommended using the NCCN Guidelines for SCLC [43,44]. The role of immunotherapy is unclear based on limited data [43-46]. If TKI is continued, ICI should be avoided due to known toxicity [45,47,48]. Consider referral to a center with expertise managing transformed SCLC.

regimens.regimen_name: Systemic Therapy ± RT for Localized Symptomatic Sites in Extensive-Stage SCLC
regimens.setting: Primary treatment for extensive-stage SCLC with localized symptomatic sites

drugs.name: Systemic therapy (platinum/etoposide ± immunotherapy per primary treatment regimens)
RT to symptomatic sites
drugs.dose: Palliative dosing (e.g., 30 Gy in 10 fractions, 20 Gy in 5 fractions, 8 Gy in 1 fraction)

regimens.notes: For extensive-stage SCLC with localized symptomatic sites including superior vena cava (SVC) syndrome, lobar obstruction, and bone metastases. Systemic therapy ± RT to symptomatic sites is recommended. If high risk of fracture due to osseous structural impairment, consider orthopedic stabilization and palliative external beam RT. For spinal cord compression: initiate steroids, systemic therapy + RT (typically sequential), see NCCN CNS Cancers guidelines. Dexamethasone 10 mg loading dose followed by 4-6 mg maintenance dose (IV or PO every 4-6 hours) [122].

regimens.regimen_name: Systemic Therapy ± Brain RT for Extensive-Stage SCLC with Brain Metastases
regimens.setting: Primary treatment for extensive-stage SCLC with brain metastases

drugs.name: Systemic therapy (platinum/etoposide ± immunotherapy per primary treatment regimens)
Brain RT (WBRT or SRS)
drugs.dose: WBRT: 30 Gy in 10 fractions; SRS per protocol

regimens.notes: For asymptomatic brain metastases: administer systemic therapy before initiating brain RT. For symptomatic brain metastases: brain RT before systemic therapy (unless immediate systemic therapy is indicated); initiate steroids. If brain metastases progress while on systemic therapy, brain RT should be initiated before completion of systemic therapy. WBRT dose: 30 Gy in 10 daily fractions. Consider adding memantine during and after RT. For patients with better prognosis (≥4 months), hippocampal-sparing WBRT using IMRT plus memantine is preferred.
